nodes.title: Generate your own timer ID
nodes.why: We return a custom numeric id so callers never depend on browser-specific timeout handles.
nodes.alternative: Return the native handle directly from setTimeout.
nodes.tradeoff: Custom ids make the API stable, but you need a registry map to translate id -> handle.

nodes.title: Delete timer before running callback
nodes.why: As soon as the timer fires, it is no longer active. Removing it keeps the registry accurate.
nodes.alternative: Only remove entries when clear is called.
nodes.tradeoff: Immediate cleanup avoids memory leaks and stale ids, with one extra map operation.

nodes.title: Store id to handle mapping
nodes.why: This is what allows myClearTimeout(id) to find and cancel the right native timer.
nodes.alternative: Keep handles in an array and search linearly.
nodes.tradeoff: Map gives O(1) lookup and delete, at the cost of a little extra memory.

nodes.title: Clear only if timer exists
nodes.why: Unknown ids should be safe no-ops, not runtime errors.
nodes.alternative: Throw when id is not found.
nodes.tradeoff: No-op behavior is friendlier for callers, but it can hide mistakes if ids are wrong.

nodes.title: Cancel every handle, then clear the map
nodes.why: clearAll should guarantee no scheduled callbacks remain and no stale entries stay in memory.
nodes.alternative: Replace the map with a new one without clearing old handles.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicitly clearing each handle is slightly more work, but behavior is correct and predictable.
